supersedes: cautilus.epic_tree_proposal.v1-draft (tree form; this realizes the R15 DAG)
status: DRAFT — R15 DAG realized over the ratified gold CLAIMS by reading the template-emitted per-claim epic facets. PENDING maintainer ratification of the epic structure (the HITL ratified verdict + proof-route, not the epic assignments).
generatedFrom: agentLabels.{primaryEpic, supportingEpics, edgeRationale, multiEpic} on each gold-set entry (template-emitted facets).
model: R15 claim DAG read from per-claim facets: each claim carries supportingEpics[] (>=1, many-to-many, acyclic). primaryEpic = R14 tree home, preserved for audit. No orphans. Epic coverage = inverse of supportingEpics; thin support is an honest recall/coverage signal, not a drawing artifact.

epics.title: Cautilus keeps agent/workflow behavior honest across prompt/skill/wrapper change
epics.userStory: As a team whose prompts/skills/models keep changing, I want to pin the behavior that matters and prove it survives every change, so that I can ship changes without silently regressing what I promised.

epics.title: The Cautilus Agent owns workflow routing, sequencing, and guardrails
epics.userStory: As a maintainer, I want the Cautilus Agent to own routing, sequencing, and guardrails, so that I do not hand-orchestrate the eval workflow step by step.

epics.title: The Cautilus Agent curates discovery packets and drives structured review
epics.userStory: As a maintainer, I want the agent to curate raw discovery packets and drive structured review, so that I review a trustworthy claim set instead of high-recall noise.

epics.title: Cautilus installs as a shared machine-level binary plus repo-local agent surface (no scaffold copy)
epics.userStory: As an adopter, I want Cautilus to install as a shared machine-level binary plus a repo-local agent surface, so that I add evaluation without copying another scaffold into my repo.

epics.title: doctor/version report setup, onboarding, agent-surface, and wiring readiness
epics.userStory: As an adopter, I want doctor/version to report setup, onboarding, and wiring readiness, so that I know exactly what to fix before a first bounded run.

epics.title: Claim discovery turns adapter-owned docs + linked Markdown into bounded proof-plan candidates (not a verdict)
epics.userStory: As a maintainer, I want discovery to turn my adapter-owned docs into bounded proof-plan candidates (not a verdict), so that I start from source-ref-backed claims rather than a blank page.

epics.title: Deterministic claim review/refresh: cluster, apply-review, refresh, status, stale-gating, model-spend gating
epics.userStory: As a maintainer, I want deterministic claim review/refresh (cluster, apply-review, refresh, status, stale/model-spend gating), so that my claim set stays current and auditable without surprise model spend.

epics.title: evaluate fixture/observation runs checked-in inputs through adapter-owned runners and evaluates the packet
epics.userStory: As a maintainer, I want evaluate fixture/observation to run checked-in inputs through adapter-owned runners and evaluate the packet, so that I get a durable, comparable verdict surface.

epics.title: Scenario proposal/attention and skill-experiment compare over preserved baseline/variant outputs
epics.userStory: As a maintainer, I want scenario proposal/attention and skill-experiment compare over preserved baseline/variant outputs, so that I can judge whether a candidate actually got better.

epics.title: Improvement search/revision is bounded by budgets, checkpoints, and blocked-readiness (bounded autonomy)
epics.userStory: As a maintainer, I want improvement search/revision bounded by budgets, checkpoints, and blocked-readiness, so that autonomy stays explicit and never runs away.

epics.title: Cautilus is proven on itself: specs, dogfood evidence, held-out validation, structured review
epics.userStory: As an evaluator of the product itself, I want Cautilus proven on itself via specs, dogfood evidence, held-out validation, and structured review, so that I can trust the promises before adopting it.

claims.edgeRationale: Pinning behavior that survives change is the apex promise (APEX); improving within explicit budgets supports bounded improvement (I1-bounded-improvement).
Repo-local checked-in agent surface is an install/ownership boundary (S1-install) that also makes evaluation behavior reproducible and owned by the declaring repo (M1-proven-on-itself).
Reproducible/reviewable/owned evaluation behavior is a proven-on-itself property (M1-proven-on-itself) conferred by the repo-local install boundary (S1-install).

Honest badge proof is the proven-on-itself promise (M1-proven-on-itself) covering the apex (APEX), with readiness (S2-readiness) and claim discovery (D1-discovery) called out as the live/proven slices.
Evaluate fixture/observation is the core evaluate loop (E1-evaluate), and skill-experiment is the scenario/experiment compare surface (E2-scenarios-experiments).
Claim discovery ships under D1-discovery and bounded improvement ships under I1-bounded-improvement, both opt-in.
Platform and git prerequisites gate install (S1-install) and are part of first-run readiness (S2-readiness).
Agent-driven setup is part of install (S1-install) carried out by the orchestrating agent (A1-orchestration).
Raw discovery packets are the discovery output (D1-discovery) that the agent curates before review (A2-curation-review).
The generated website report serves proven-on-itself reporting (M1-proven-on-itself), while the boundary that the renderer is not required for inspection supports readiness/onboarding (S2-readiness).
Turning behavior evidence into a reviewable decision surface is the apex product claim (APEX) realized by the evaluate loop (E1-evaluate).
Turning transcript judgment into a reproducible reopenable packet is the evaluate-loop value (E1-evaluate) demonstrated as self-proof (M1-proven-on-itself).
The dev/app surface split structures the core evaluate surface (E1-evaluate) across which scenarios and experiments are run (E2-scenarios-experiments).
Holding a scenario out of tuning is the bounded-improvement held-out discipline (I1-bounded-improvement) over a protected scenario (E2-scenarios-experiments).
Durable reopenable evidence files are an evaluate-loop output (E1-evaluate) embodying the apex packet-first stance (APEX).

Separating iteration from protected validation is the held-out improvement discipline (I1-bounded-improvement); keeping evidence reopenable from files is the packet-first evaluate property (E1-evaluate).
Two entry points sharing one host-owned adapter is an orchestration boundary (A1-orchestration) grounded in the host-owned install surface (S1-install).
Init installing the repo-local agent under a fixed path is an install fact (S1-install) that provisions the agent orchestration surface (A1-orchestration).
The minimum host-repo shape defines the install footprint (S1-install) and what doctor checks for readiness (S2-readiness).
Machine-readable packets plus reopenable readable views are the evaluate decision surface (E1-evaluate) consumed during structured review (A2-curation-review).
S1-install is supported because wiring a runnable path is part of completing adapter install/bootstrap.
S2-readiness is supported because command discovery is part of the binary's readiness/orientation surface for agent runtimes.
A1-orchestration is supported because no-input agent orientation feeds the Cautilus Agent's routing decisions.
S1-install is supported because agent-surface verification confirms the repo-local agent install landed.
E1-evaluate is supported because first_bounded_run seeds the starter evaluate fixture/observation loop.
D2-review-refresh is supported because the status summary feeds the review/refresh branch decision.
D2-review-refresh is supported because review-input clusters feed the deterministic claim review/refresh flow.
E1-evaluate is supported because the prepared worktrees back a baseline-versus-candidate evaluation run.
D1-discovery is supported because --example-input makes expected packet shapes discoverable to operators.
Single-epic: the shipped cautilus evaluate surface is the E1-evaluate front door.
E2-scenarios-experiments is supported because the dev/skill and app surfaces are the targets for scenario and skill-experiment work.
A1-orchestration is supported because reporting status and asking the next branch is part of the agent's orchestration loop.
Resumable durable packets serve agent orchestration handoff (A1-orchestration) and the apex packet-first reopenable-evidence stance (APEX).
Agent curation is the curation/review epic (A2-curation-review) operating on discovery output (D1-discovery).
Preserving the scope-of-proof distinction is an honest self-proof discipline (M1-proven-on-itself) recorded in the eval packet (E1-evaluate).
Agent curation before trust is the curation/review epic (A2-curation-review) gating the discovery output (D1-discovery).
Agent-facing-first interfaces with HTML as a mirror is an ownership/source-of-truth boundary supporting agent orchestration (A1-orchestration) and the apex packet-first stance (APEX).
First-class adapters/reports/review/compare artifacts is the structured-review boundary (A2-curation-review) that compare artifacts under experiments rely on (E2-scenarios-experiments).

Splitting proof layers is an apex design principle (APEX) that routes evaluator-dependent behavior through evaluate (E1-evaluate) and gates improvement on an explicit proof surface (I1-bounded-improvement).
An end-to-end fresh-consumer onboard smoke is self-proof evidence (M1-proven-on-itself) of the install/adoption path (S1-install).
A1-orchestration is supported because the orientation packet drives the agent's branch choices and routing.
A2-curation-review is supported because the extraction-input packet is the agent's input for curating extracted claims.
D1-discovery is supported because apply-extraction composes the discovery proof plan from extracted claims.
D2-review-refresh is supported because deterministic non-LLM rejection keeps the claim set auditable without surprise model spend.
A2-curation-review is supported because review-input is the agent's curated review surface.
D2-review-refresh is supported because eval planning consumes reviewed claim packets from the review/refresh flow.
D1-discovery is supported because live-targets discovers the catalog of live consumer instances.
E1-evaluate is supported because the prepared request batch feeds the live evaluation primitive.
E1-evaluate is supported because the batch primitive executes the live evaluation runs.
S1-install is supported because the consumer-owned launch/auth/runtime wiring is the adapter ownership boundary established at install.
E1-evaluate is supported because persona prompt shaping happens inside the live evaluation loop.
E1-evaluate is supported because execution (run-scenarios) is the live evaluation run primitive.
E2-scenarios-experiments is supported because the exposed inspect commands target scenario normalization families.
A2-curation-review is supported because review-conversations curates evidence for structured operator review.
M1-proven-on-itself is supported because these are the repo's own dogfood smoke variants proving the product on itself.
M1-proven-on-itself is supported because the boundary clarifies what level of self-proof the dogfood variants do and do not provide.
E1-evaluate is supported because review variants produce a per-variant verdict surface over an evaluation report.
D2-review-refresh is supported because review-feedback normalization captures review-learning evidence that informs refresh.
A1-orchestration is supported because curating the skeleton into the expected workflow is part of the agent owning workflow routing and sequencing.
D2-review-refresh is supported because the binary owning state transitions covers claim review and refresh state transitions.
A2-curation-review is supported because LLM-backed claim review is the agent's curation/review responsibility.
A1-orchestration is supported because owning extraction and status is part of the agent orchestrating the eval workflow.
A1-orchestration is supported because the agent selecting refresh is a routing decision the agent owns.
I1-bounded-improvement is supported because separate budget confirmation keeps spend bounded.
M1-proven-on-itself is supported because the no-model reproducible floor enables CI regeneration and control tests for self-proof.
D2-review-refresh is supported because refresh plans and claim-state validation are part of review/refresh.
D1-discovery is supported because this boundary defines what the discovery binary does not do.
A2-curation-review is supported because extracting claims and reconciling evidence are curation/review responsibilities.
S1-install is supported because keeping the binary out of host-specific runtime is part of the shared-binary plus repo-local agent split.
D1-discovery is supported because agent-primary extraction is the discovery extraction path.
D2-review-refresh is supported because refresh planning and carry-forward are review/refresh responsibilities.
I1-bounded-improvement is supported because bounding re-extraction cost keeps spend bounded.
M1-proven-on-itself is supported because the reproducible floor enables CI regeneration and control tests for self-proof.
E1-evaluate is supported because the gold set is an eval fixture measuring extraction quality.
A1-orchestration is supported because not auto-launching expensive runs is an agent guardrail.
D1-discovery is supported because the deterministic provider-neutral binary is the discovery engine boundary.
A1-orchestration is supported because the clean binary/skill boundary lets the agent orchestrate without host-specific imports.
A1-orchestration is supported because seeding hints is part of the agent's onboarding orchestration before discovery.
S2-readiness is supported because checking adapter availability and claim-state artifacts is readiness orientation work.
A2-curation-review is supported because the agent doing the model-backed extraction work is the curation pass that drives structured review.
D2-review-refresh is supported because apply-extraction's fingerprint computation and binary-owned packet assembly underpin the deterministic review/refresh chain.
D1-discovery is supported because the binary-owned skeleton composition is part of the discovery proof-plan packet assembly.
D2-review-refresh is supported because the no-model-call determinism also covers the review/refresh commands' deterministic guarantee.
A1-orchestration is supported because the budget-confirmation gate is part of the agent workflow guardrails the Cautilus Agent owns.
D1-discovery is supported because the adapter ownership boundary governs how discovery scopes a consumer repo's sources.
D1-discovery is supported because the added claim-shape prompts directly raise discovery recall.
S1-install is supported because keeping repo-specific knowledge solely in the adapter is what keeps the binary portable across installs.
A2-curation-review is supported because folding routing into extraction is part of producing a review-ready, curated claim set.
E1-evaluate is supported because correct proof routing determines which claims become evaluable, comparable verdict-surface candidates.
E1-evaluate is supported because routing agent-behavior claims to cautilus-eval determines what actually gets evaluated for a verdict.
S1-install is supported because keeping the epic catalog adapter-supplied rather than hardcoded preserves the portable shared-binary install boundary.
D1-discovery is supported because the no-silent-drop uncertainty rule preserves discovery recall.
S1-install is supported because per-repo adapter divergence yielding distinct hashes is part of the portable per-repo configuration boundary.
D2-review-refresh is supported because binary-owned fingerprint computation is the identity mechanism the refresh chain relies on.
A2-curation-review is supported because the recorded rejection audit makes the curated claim set trustworthy and auditable for review.
D1-discovery is supported because the heuristic sourceRefs shape governs the default discovery output's packet structure.
D2-review-refresh is supported because these review/evidence/lifecycle labels drive how claims carry forward and refresh.
A2-curation-review is supported because surfacing unmapped epics for review rather than rejecting is a curation/review behavior.
A2-curation-review is supported because the exclusion-with-auditability rule shapes which claims the structured review pass spends budget on.
I1-bounded-improvement is supported because the budget gates guarding any actual evaluator run keep autonomy bounded.
D1-discovery is supported because the source-hash drift check guards the integrity of the discovered claim packet at apply time.
D1-discovery is supported because recording extraction-time hashes preserves the discovered packet's auditability against later drift.
M1-proven-on-itself is supported because the heuristic baseline mode is the labeled control against which agent extraction is measured.
E2-scenarios-experiments is supported because the bounded comparison harness is the compare-over-baseline-variant mechanism for judging the agent candidate.
A1-orchestration is supported because verifying the agent flow over the skill surface targets the agent's own orchestration behavior.
A2-curation-review is supported because guaranteeing no fabricated excerpt survives is what makes the curated claim set trustworthy.
S2-readiness is supported because the progressive-disclosure split keeps broad command discovery and help on the binary side.
S1-install is supported because the no-new-hardcoding control keeps the binary portable across consumer installs.
D2-review-refresh is supported because the state_path caveat concerns the claims:refresh:all push-gate refresh behavior.

M1 owns the present-tense-prose authoring discipline as a proven-on-itself rule; D1-discovery is supported because the rule exists specifically to keep stale narrative prose from being extracted as claims during discovery.

M1 owns the decision-recording discipline; D1-discovery is supported because heading-named decision sections are the non-claim convention that lets discovery deterministically exclude these decisions.
D1-discovery owns the non-claim-convention exclusion during extraction; D2-review-refresh is supported because stale-claim extraction is what the review/refresh loop must catch.
M1 owns the proven-on-itself spec discipline that keeps cross-cutting concerns visible; APEX is supported because the cross-cutting concerns sit on the canonical promise model that APEX anchors.
APEX is the home for Cautilus's product responsibility boundary; D1-discovery is supported because the machine-readable packet/provenance/next-work-route artifacts are the discovery output surface.

A1-orchestration owns the agent's workflow-branch routing role; A2-curation-review is supported because interpreting the packet is part of how the agent curates and routes the discovery/review output.

A1-orchestration owns the skill-routing behavior of discovering support/specdown before doing the work; D1-discovery is supported because find-skills inventory routing is part of the discovery surface this rule governs.

S2-readiness owns the actionable-error doctor/CLI contract that guides an operator to the new path; D2-review-refresh is supported because schema/subcommand migration is part of keeping the review/refresh surface current.

D2-review-refresh owns the catalog deprecation/normalization default; S1-install is supported because forced consumer migration vs silent normalization directly affects the adopter's install/upgrade experience.
M1-proven-on-itself owns the parity-gate quality discipline; D2-review-refresh is supported because the proposalKey parity gate guards the deterministic claim/proposal review surface.
M1-proven-on-itself owns the doc-quality gate discipline; A2-curation-review is supported because rendered-preview reading is part of the structured document HITL review loop.

A1-orchestration owns the agent's command-sequencing/parallelization guardrail; D2-review-refresh is supported because the named hazard concerns the discover claims status artifact in the review/refresh flow.
S1-install is the home for starter-kit packaging; E2-scenarios-experiments is supported because the copied input.json is the canonical scenario fixture it mirrors.
S1-install owns the packaged starter-kit copy; M1-proven-on-itself is supported because byte-identity drift tests are the self-proving gate that enforces the duplication.
M1-proven-on-itself owns the dependency-discipline rule; S1-install is supported because reusing run-install-smoke.mjs concerns the install smoke surface.
S1-install owns the shipped-binary product surface that Go represents; A1-orchestration is supported because the JS helper/runtime seam carries the bounded agent-runtime orchestration.
S1-install owns the standalone-binary install surface; A1-orchestration is supported because the Cautilus Agent half of the promised product surface owns workflow routing.
S1-install owns the shipped Go CLI surface; S2-readiness is supported because operator-expected stable cautilus commands surface through doctor/CLI discovery.
A1-orchestration owns the thin orchestration/helper runtime seam in scripts/agent-runtime; S1-install is supported because the boundary defines what stays out of the shipped binary surface.
S1-install owns the Go promotion/ownership-cut rule; E1-evaluate is supported because consumer-owned probe/launch/auth/runtime wiring is the host-execution boundary of the evaluate surface.

S2-readiness owns the cautilus doctor commands / CLI reference discovery surface that the criterion references; S1-install is supported because the criterion governs which seams become shipped Go binary surface.

E1-evaluate owns cautilus evaluate live as a sub-execution surface; D1-discovery is supported because cautilus discover live-targets is a discovery surface; S1-install is supported because Go promotion adds the seam to the shipped binary.

E1-evaluate owns the live-invocation ownership boundary via consumer_command_template; D1-discovery is supported because kind:command discovery and the consumer probe catalog are the discovery side of the same boundary.
S1-install owns the Go CLI shipped surface decision; S2-readiness is supported because 'a surface an operator would actually look for' is the operator-discovery readiness signal.
S2-readiness owns the operator-facing CLI entrypoint readiness; S1-install is supported because the trigger results in promoting the seam to the shipped binary.
S2-readiness owns CLI subcommand discovery/documentation; S1-install is supported because the documented subcommand becomes shipped binary surface.
S2-readiness owns binary-level command discovery / help text; S1-install is supported because binary-level smoke is part of the shipped install surface.
S1-install owns the packaged-skill-tree sync behavior; A1-orchestration is supported because the rewritten references live under the Cautilus Agent skill surface.
S1-install owns the starter-kit parity test; E2-scenarios-experiments is supported because the mirrored fixture is the scenario-proposals input.
M1-proven-on-itself owns the self-review-before-commit spec discipline; A2-curation-review is supported because the three-question self-premortem is a structured review pass.

openQuestions: Epic facets are now template-emitted per claim (agentLabels.{primaryEpic, supportingEpics, edgeRationale, multiEpic}); this DAG reads them verbatim instead of hand-routing an EDGE_MAP from claimSemanticGroup. The slice-3 conclusion (the template should emit per-claim facets) is realized — see build-epic-dag.mjs for the prior hand-routed approach it supersedes.

Ratification caveat: the HITL session (hitl-reextract-v2head-20260618) ratified the maintainer VERDICT (claim / not-a-claim) and the proof-route, NOT the epic assignments. primaryEpic / supportingEpics / edgeRationale are agent-assigned at extraction and carried forward; the epic STRUCTURE is pending a maintainer ratification pass.

Thin epics (<=2 total support) are reported as honest coverage signals, not drawing artifacts; a per-track DAG will surface more thin epics than the combined roll-up because audience segmentation removes the other track's support.
